title: Apocalypse Forever? Post-political Populism and the Spectre of Climate Change
author: Swyngedouw

opening_move.description: The paper opens with a paradox: climate change is seemingly politicized as never before (on policy agendas, global consensus, mass media attention), yet the same period has seen the consolidation of a post-political and post-democratic condition (per Žižek, Rancière, Mouffe) in which genuine political contestation is evacuated from public life. The paper asks: how can these two apparently contradictory developments coexist? The resolution is the paper's core argument.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Hegemonic climate change discourse is not a form of politicization but a key mechanism through which a post-political, post-democratic configuration is forged and entrenched. Genuine democratic environmental politics requires reclaiming the space of dissensus and naming proper political subjects.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper synthesizes post-foundational political philosophy (Rancière, Žižek, Mouffe, Badiou) with political ecology and geography. It takes the post-political condition at face value as diagnosed by these thinkers and extends their framework to the environmental domain, arguing that climate change is a primary arena through which post-political depoliticization is accomplished. It draws on Laclau's theory of populism and Žižek's Lacanian analysis of empty signifiers and commodity fetishism to read CO2 as the 'objet petit a' of environmental politics.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Discourse analysis of climate change media coverage (headlines from The Independent, 2006), policy documents (Kyoto Protocol, UK Pathways to 2050 equivalent), political speeches (Prince Charles), corporate communications (BP rebranding), and Al Gore's film An Inconvenient Truth. Enumeration of CO2 derivatives market growth (zero to 463 million tons, 2005–2009) as illustration.
data_sources: European Climate Exchange CO2 futures data
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Lacanian-Žižekian analysis of ideological fantasy (apocalyptic imaginary, CO2 as objet petit a); Rancièrian analysis of the partition of the sensible and post-democratic governance; Laclauian analysis of populist empty signifiers; political economy of carbon commodification (Bumpus and Liverman).
epistemic_mode.notes: The empirical material (media headlines, market data, policy documents) is illustrative — it demonstrates the characteristics of the populist-post-political climate discourse rather than constituting independent evidence for the theoretical argument. The argument is primarily derived from the post-foundational political philosophy tradition. However, the CO2 derivatives market data and policy document analysis do specific work (demonstrating commodification and techno-managerial governance) that goes beyond pure illustration. Classified as normative_with_qualitative_empirical with illustrative evidence.

case_type.case_name: Hegemonic climate change discourse (Al Gore's An Inconvenient Truth, Kyoto Protocol, UK climate policy)

confidence_notes: The conclusion_strong label is appropriate because the paper concludes with an explicit prescriptive appeal: 'there is an urgent need for different stories and fictions ... This requires foregrounding and naming different socio-environmental futures and recognizing conflict, difference and struggle' — a positive normative vision of recovered agonistic democratic politics, not merely an open diagnosis. The case_function is 'illustrative_and_generative' because the analysis of climate change discourse both illustrates the post-political condition (already theorized by Rancière/Žižek) AND generates the specific insight that environmentalism is not merely an expression of but a primary mechanism for forging post-political configurations. The seven-characteristic analysis of climate change populism (universal threat, empty people, externalized enemy, techno-managerial fix, anti-left alignment, hegemonic appropriation of demands, no proper name) constitutes a systematic analytical contribution beyond the framework. The epistemic mode is normative_with_qualitative_empirical rather than fully_normative because the discourse analysis of media headlines and the market data do real empirical-illustrative work in mapping the phenomenon.
